model.label: MiMo-V2-Pro
model.description: MiMo • The flagship foundation for the agentic era, built for complex, long-horizon, multi-step agentic tasks.

pick.rationale: Michigan holds a razor-thin analytical edge as the #1 overall seed with KenPom/Torvik rank #2 compared to Arizona's #3, featuring slightly superior defensive efficiency (rank 2 vs 3) and a marginally better adjusted efficiency margin (38.38 vs 37.56). Both teams share identical 31-2 records and elite profiles on both ends of the floor, making this essentially a coin-flip matchup between two championship-caliber programs. Michigan's Big Ten championship pedigree and slightly more favorable defensive metrics give them the narrowest of edges, though Arizona's balanced attack and tournament experience under Tommy Lloyd make this an extremely competitive Final Four contest.

reasoningStep.title: Arizona vs Michigan Final Four Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Two elite #1 seeds with nearly identical analytical profiles meet in a classic Final Four showdown. Michigan holds a marginal edge in overall efficiency metrics while Arizona counters with elite balance on both ends.
evidence: KenPom rankings: Michigan #2 (38.38 adjEM) vs Arizona #3 (37.56 adjEM) - razor-thin 0.82 point difference
Torvik rankings: Michigan #2 vs Arizona #3 with nearly identical offensive and defensive efficiency profiles
Both teams share 31-2 records demonstrating elite consistency throughout the season
Michigan defensive efficiency rank 2 (88.4 adjD) vs Arizona rank 3 (89.7 adjD) - minimal defensive gap
Michigan offensive efficiency rank 8 (126.7 adjO) vs Arizona rank 7 (127.2 adjO) - Arizona holds slight offensive edge
Michigan's Yaxel Lendeborg dealing with low-ankle sprain but expected to play; Arizona's Koa Peat previously injured but returned for tournament
Both teams have navigated challenging paths to the Final Four with Michigan beating Iowa State and Arizona defeating Purdue in Elite 8

By Alan Rubenstein | Share on Facebook Share on X Share on Reddit Copy Article Add us as a preferred source Arizona Wildcats forward Koa Peat (10) awaits for an inbound pass during the game against Kansas Jayhawks inside Allen Fieldhouse on Feb. 9, 2026. | Evert Nelson/The Capital-Journal / USA TODAY NETWORK via Imagn Images Arizona was without freshmen forwards Dwayne Aristode for the entire game and Koa Peat for the second half in the loss to Texas Tech on Saturday. Aristode was out with an illness and Peat sat out the second half with a leg injury, according to Arizona head coach Tommy Lloyd in the post game. --> Arizona was without freshmen forwards Dwayne Aristode for the entire game and Koa Peat for the second half in the loss to Texas Tech on Saturday. Aristode was out with an illness and Peat sat out the second half with a leg injury, according to Arizona head coach Tommy Lloyd in the post game. Peat had consecutive games below 10 points for the first time this season. Playing 11 minutes, Peat scored two points without taking a shot from the field and had one rebound as the game was tied at 32 at halftime. Peat has a lower leg injury that Arizona trainer Justin Kokoskie and doctors will examine. --> Peat had consecutive games below 10 points for the first time this season. When further asked if Aristode will play against BYU on Wednesday, Lloyd replied, "I don't know for sure...but I think there's a chance." result.text: Depth concerns popping up for Arizona Wildcats men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues | Arizona Desert Swarm Skip to main content SB Nation SB Nation logo An Arizona Wildcats Community Arizona Desert Swarm March 19, 2026 Arizona Desert Swarm Notifications Notifications The Feed Sections Arizona Basketball Arizona Football Arizona Softball Arizona Recruiting Arizona Soccer Arizona Volleyball Arizona Baseball Arizona Golf Arizona Gymnastics Arizona in the Pros Betting FanDuel College Football Odds FanDuel College Basketball Odds Arizona Football Odds Arizona Basketball Odds College Football Picks and Predictions College Basketball Picks and Predictions Shop About Navigation Drawer Login / Sign Up close Close Search The Feed Sections Expand Arizona Basketball Arizona Football Arizona Softball Arizona Recruiting Arizona Soccer Arizona Volleyball Arizona Baseball Arizona Golf Arizona Gymnastics Arizona in the Pros Betting Expand FanDuel College Football Odds FanDuel College Basketball Odds Arizona Football Odds Arizona Basketball Odds College Football Picks and Predictions College Basketball Picks and Predictions Shop About Community Guidelines Newsletters All communities Facebook Twitter Youtube RSS Arizona Desert Swarm Depth concerns popping up for Arizona men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues Notifications Notifications Arizona Basketball Depth concerns popping up for Arizona men’s basketball as toughest stretch of schedule continues by Brian Pedersen Feb 16, 2026, 10:00 AM UTC Facebook Link TUCSON, ARIZONA - FEBRUARY 14: Motiejus Krivas #13 and Koa Peat #10 of the Arizona Wildcats walk off the court after losing to the Texas Tech Red Raiders 78-75 in overtime at McKale Center at ALKEME Arena on February 14, 2026 in Tucson, Arizona. (Photo by Chris Coduto/Getty Images) Getty Images In Tommy Lloyd’s first season, it was Kerr Kriisa . The second year it was Oumar Ballo. Then Henri Veesaar in 2023-24 and Motiejus Krivas last season. Injuries and absences are a part of every sport, and Arizona has not been immune from this. It just hadn’t happened to the current Wildcats team until Saturday, when Dwayne Aristode sat out the overtime loss to Texas Tech with an illness and Koa Peat suffered a lower body injury and missed the second half. “If you think you’re going to go through a season unscathed, you’ve never done this before,” Lloyd said Saturday after the UA lost a second straight game. “You just got to be honest with yourself. We’re all human. I mean, the season’s been long, obviously you can see some injuries and illnesses are starting to pile up and catch up with us a little bit. We’ll bounce back. And hopefully we will be fully loaded when we need to be.” Both Aristode and Peat could miss the next game, Wednesday at home against No. 22 BYU. (Photo by Chris Coduto/Getty Images) Getty Images In Tommy Lloyd’s first season, it was Kerr Kriisa . The second year it was Oumar Ballo. Then Henri Veesaar in 2023-24 and Motiejus Krivas last season. Injuries and absences are a part of every sport, and Arizona has not been immune from this. It just hadn’t happened to the current Wildcats team until Saturday, when Dwayne Aristode sat out the overtime loss to Texas Tech with an illness and Koa Peat suffered a lower body injury and missed the second half. “If you think you’re going to go through a season unscathed, you’ve never done this before,” Lloyd said Saturday after the UA lost a second straight game. “You just got to be honest with yourself. We’re all human. I mean, the season’s been long, obviously you can see some injuries and illnesses are starting to pile up and catch up with us a little bit. We’ll bounce back. And hopefully we will be fully loaded when we need to be.” Both Aristode and Peat could miss the next game, Wednesday at home against No. 22 BYU.
